title: Charlemagne crowned Holy Roman Emperor
summary: On Christmas Day 800, during the Mass in Old St Peter's Basilica in Rome, Pope Leo III placed a crown on the head of the Frankish king Charles, called the Great, and the assembled Romans hailed him as Imperator Romanorum. For the first time in more than three centuries a western ruler bore the title of Roman emperor. The act linked Latin Christianity to a Germanic kingdom that stretched from the Pyrenees to the Elbe, and it laid the foundation of the medieval and early modern idea of a Holy Roman Empire in the west.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The line of western Roman emperors had ended in 476, when the German general Odoacer deposed the child Romulus Augustulus and sent the imperial insignia to Constantinople. For three centuries thereafter, the only emperor in Christendom sat on the Bosphorus. In the west, the successor kingdoms of the Franks, Visigoths, Ostrogoths, Vandals and Lombards ruled as kings, sometimes as patricians of Rome or exarchs of Ravenna, but never as emperors. That the imperial name might one day return to the Latin west would have struck any sixth-century bishop as impossible.

Two long developments changed that. First, the Frankish kingdom under the Carolingian family rose to overwhelming preponderance north of the Alps. Charles's grandfather, the mayor of the palace Charles Martel, had halted an Arab army near Poitiers in 732; his father Pippin the Short had, with papal blessing, deposed the last Merovingian in 751 and been anointed king by Pope Stephen II three years later. Charles himself, born around 748 and sole ruler of the Franks from 771, had annexed Lombard Italy in 774, crushed the Saxons in a brutal thirty-year campaign, seized Bavaria in 788, and destroyed the Avar khaganate on the middle Danube in 796. By 800 he ruled the largest western polity since Rome itself.

Second, the papacy had drifted decisively out of the Byzantine orbit. The iconoclast controversy of the eighth century, in which emperors at Constantinople proscribed the veneration of religious images, had alienated the Latin popes; the Byzantine tax on Italian papal estates in the 730s had turned alienation into anger. In 754 Stephen II crossed the Alps to seek Frankish protection and returned home with Pippin's guarantee of the temporal territories that became the Papal States. When Empress Irene of Athens, mother of Constantine VI, blinded her son in 797 and had herself acclaimed as basileus, western clerics could argue with some plausibility that the imperial throne at Constantinople was vacant, since a woman could not be true emperor of the Romans.

Into this atmosphere came Pope Leo III, elected in 795 without the caliphate of Charles's approval, and never wholly secure in his own see. In April 799 Roman aristocrats loyal to his predecessor Hadrian I attacked him during a procession, tried to blind and mutilate him, and shut him in a monastery. Escaping to Charles's court at Paderborn in Saxony, Leo persuaded the Frankish king to restore him. Charles arrived in Rome in November 800, held a synod at St Peter's on 1 December to investigate the charges against the pope, and on 23 December accepted Leo's public oath of purgation. Two days later, on Christmas morning, the great ceremony took place.

sections.heading: What happened

image.caption: The Coronation of Charlemagne, Vatican fresco after Raphael, c. 1516-17.
image.credit: Raphael and workshop, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.body:
The Mass began at dawn in Old St Peter's, the fourth-century basilica raised by Constantine over the tomb of the apostle. Charles, in his early fifties, wore Roman patrician dress at his own preference and knelt before the confessio of St Peter to pray. The pope's contemporary biographer, in the Liber Pontificalis, records what happened next in words that are still the earliest written account: "After these things, on the day of the Nativity of our Lord Jesus Christ, all of them being assembled in the basilica of the blessed apostle Peter, the venerable and salutary pontiff, with his own hands, crowned him with a most precious crown. Then all the faithful Romans, seeing what great defence and love he bore towards the holy Roman church and its Vicar, at the direction of God and of the blessed Peter, key-bearer of the kingdom of heaven, cried with one voice: To Charles, the most pious Augustus, crowned by God, the great and peaceful Emperor of the Romans, life and victory!" The pope then, according to the Frankish Royal Annals, prostrated himself before the newly crowned emperor in the manner of ancient imperial protocol.

Charles's own biographer Einhard, writing at Aachen a generation later, added a famous complication. According to Einhard's Vita Karoli Magni, Charles later told friends that he would never have entered the church that day had he known what Leo intended. Modern scholars have divided sharply on this claim. Some, following Louis Halphen and Peter Classen, take it as a genuine expression of the emperor's political embarrassment: the coronation might implicitly assert papal power to make and unmake emperors, and it certainly complicated relations with Constantinople. Others, notably Roger Collins, argue that Einhard's disclaimer is a literary topos of humility, and that Charles and Leo had planned the ceremony together, with the pope taking the initiative to shield his royal patron from the charge of usurpation.

Whatever the truth about surprise, no one at the time was in doubt about the substance. Charles was now, in his own chancery's title of 1 January 801, "Charles, most serene Augustus, crowned by God, great and peace-bringing emperor, governing the Roman empire, and by God's mercy king of the Franks and Lombards". Coins from Aachen soon bore the imperial title. Ambassadors were dispatched to Constantinople, where the news was received with astonishment and offence: Byzantine officials referred to Charles thereafter with pointed courtesy as "basileus of the Franks," not of the Romans. It would take twelve years, a series of embassies, and the deposition of Irene in 802, before the Byzantine emperor Michael I in 812 was willing, in exchange for the return of Venice and parts of Dalmatia, to acknowledge Charles as an emperor, though still not as emperor of the Romans.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences

image.caption: Interior of the Palatine Chapel at Aachen, Charlemagne's imperial capital, modelled on San Vitale in Ravenna.

sections.body:
Charles's own reaction was to work hard to make the title mean something. He continued to reside not in Rome but at Aachen, the frontier villa in the Rhineland he had transformed into a Roman-styled capital with a great octagonal palatine chapel modelled on San Vitale in Ravenna. There he gathered a court of scholars, most famously the Northumbrian Alcuin of York, the Lombard grammarian Paul the Deacon and the Visigothic bishop Theodulf of Orléans, and set in motion what modern historians call the Carolingian Renaissance. A standardised liturgy, a corrected Vulgate Bible, a new script known as Carolingian minuscule, and copies of ancient Latin authors that survive today only because Carolingian monasteries recopied them are the enduring intellectual legacy of those years.

Administratively, Charlemagne divided his empire into some three hundred counties, each held by a count answerable to travelling inspectors called missi dominici. General assemblies met yearly to promulgate the capitularies through which he legislated. In the church, monastic and cathedral schools were reformed to produce a literate clergy capable of preaching in the vernacular. The Ordinatio Imperii of 817, issued by his son Louis the Pious, tried to fix a stable imperial succession, but the treaty of Verdun of 843, dividing the empire among the three surviving grandsons Lothair, Louis the German and Charles the Bald, effectively ended Carolingian imperial unity.

The imperial idea itself, however, proved much more durable than the Frankish state. When the Saxon king Otto I was crowned emperor by Pope John XII in 962, he explicitly presented himself as heir to Charlemagne, and the polity that emerged, the Holy Roman Empire of the German Nation, would last until 1806. Successive coronations of German kings in Rome, of the last, Charles V, in 1530 at Bologna by Clement VII, all traced their theatre back to 25 December 800. The papacy's role in that first coronation likewise anchored centuries of debate over whether the pope had the power to translate the empire from one people to another, a claim developed by canonists in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries and bitterly resisted by German emperors from Barbarossa to Frederick II.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The coronation reoriented European history. It fused the Latin church, the Germanic warrior aristocracy, and the memory of Rome into a single political imagination that would shape western Christendom until the Reformation and beyond. The very phrase Holy Roman Empire is unthinkable without the moment when Leo III raised the crown over Charles's head. In the ninth century that phrase was still absent, but by the reign of Frederick Barbarossa in the twelfth century it had become the standard title of the western Reich, an implicit claim that a Christian imperium had never truly ended.

The long-term consequences for east-west relations were more painful. The coronation formalised the divergence between a Greek and a Latin Christendom, each claiming a Roman emperor and each regarding the other's claim as illegitimate. Byzantine chroniclers such as Theophanes the Confessor recorded the news of 800 with cold contempt. Latin churchmen for their part came to speak more freely of Greek schismatics. When the mutual excommunications of 1054 tore the two churches apart, the political ground had been prepared for two and a half centuries.

Modern historians continue to debate what exactly Charles thought he was doing. Was the empire he now ruled meant to be a revived western Rome, a Christian Roman empire in a new key, or a Frankish super-monarchy with a useful classical title? Recent work, notably by Janet Nelson and Rosamond McKitterick, has emphasised that Charles thought consciously in Roman terms, from the layout of his palace at Aachen to the Latin poetry of his court, but that he also remained recognisably a Frankish king ruling through Frankish institutions. The coronation caught this ambiguity in a single Christmas morning ceremony, and the ambiguity, as much as the ceremony itself, is what gave the event its extraordinary generative power.

worldContext.text: In Baghdad the fifth Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid (r. 786 to 809), now at the height of his powers, ruled from the round Madinat al-Salam that his grandfather al-Mansur had founded in 762 on the Tigris. The caliphate under Harun and his powerful Barmakid viziers, above all Yahya ibn Khalid and his sons Ja'far and al-Fadl, was at the peak of its cultural and economic influence, and the House of Wisdom (Bayt al-Hikma) at Baghdad was beginning to translate Greek, Sanskrit, and Middle Persian works into Arabic. Diplomatic tradition, preserved above all in the Frankish sources such as Einhard's Vita Karoli and the Royal Annals, records that Harun and Charlemagne exchanged embassies and gifts; among the objects sent to Aachen was an Asian elephant named Abul-Abbas that reached the Frankish court in 802 and lived until 810. In 803 Harun would abruptly disgrace and execute the Barmakids in one of the most consequential political ruptures of the caliphate. In North Africa the recently founded Aghlabid emirate of Ifriqiya, established under Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab in 800 by Harun himself, was consolidating in what is now Tunisia and would soon begin its conquest of Byzantine Sicily.

image.caption: Marble mihrab from the al-Khasiki Mosque in Baghdad, commissioned by the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid in 192 AH (807 CE). Iraq Museum, Baghdad.
image.credit: Osama Shukir Muhammed Amin FRCP(Glasg), via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In China the Tang dynasty had recovered institutionally from the catastrophic An Lushan Rebellion of 755 to 763 but never fully regained its earlier military reach or population; the northern provinces from Youzhou to Weibo were now ruled by semi-independent military governors (jiedushi). Emperor Dezong (r. 779 to 805) ruled from Chang'an, whose gridded plan and Western Market drew Sogdian, Persian, Arab, Turkic, and Nestorian Christian merchants; the celebrated Nestorian Stele would be raised there in 781. In Japan the young Emperor Kanmu had just moved the imperial capital to Heian-kyo (modern Kyoto) in 794, opening the four-century Heian era; the monks Saicho and Kukai were about to leave for Tang China to bring back the Tendai and Shingon esoteric Buddhist schools. In Korea the kingdom of Silla under Aejang ruled the peninsula, and the Balhae (Parhae) kingdom to its north, founded by a Goguryeo aristocrat, sent regular embassies to both Tang and Japan. On the Central Asian frontier the Tibetan Empire under Trisong Detsen was at the peak of its extraordinary expansion, briefly occupying the Tang capital Chang'an in 763 and controlling much of Gansu and the Tarim Basin.

image.caption: Standing portrait of the Tang emperor Taizong (r. 626 to 649), founder of the dynasty's high-water period, in a later imperial copy at the National Palace Museum, Taipei.
image.credit: Unknown Chinese artist, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: The Pala king Dharmapala (r. c. 775 to 812) ruled Bengal and Bihar and had recently placed a client, Chakrayudha, on the throne of Kannauj, provoking a renewed round in the three-way tripartite struggle with the Pratiharas of Rajasthan (under Vatsaraja and Nagabhata II) and the Rashtrakutas of the Deccan (under Dhruva and his son Govinda III) for supremacy in northern India. The Buddhist mahavihara of Vikramashila in Bihar was newly founded under Pala patronage, complementing the older Nalanda, and both institutions would train generations of scholars whose Vajrayana Buddhism would soon travel to Tibet. Further west the Abbasid caliphate governed the Sindh and Multan valleys through client governors, and in Central Asia the Iranian Samanid dynasty of Transoxiana under Ahmad ibn Asad was rising under Baghdad's patronage. In the south the Rashtrakuta king Govinda III was extending Deccan power as far south as Kanchi, and at Ellora his father Krishna I had recently completed the extraordinary rock-cut Kailasa Temple.

image.caption: Bronze Buddha calling the earth to witness, from Nalanda, Pala period, c. 9th to 10th century. Indian Museum, Kolkata.
image.credit: Anandajoti Bhikkhu / Photo Dharma, via Wikimedia Commons

worldContext.text: In West Africa the Wagadu kingdom of Ghana, whose capital most modern archaeologists identify with the ruins at Koumbi Saleh in modern Mauritania, was consolidating its regional power through control of the Sahel entrepots of the trans-Saharan gold trade; caravans from the Bambuk gold fields on the upper Senegal reached across the desert to Berber and Arab merchants who exchanged Saharan salt for gold. The Berber Rustamid imamate at Tahert in modern Algeria, founded in 776 by Abd al-Rahman ibn Rustam, practised an Ibadi variant of Islam and traded actively with the sub-Saharan gold fields. To the east, in Christian Nubia, the kingdoms of Makuria (with its capital at Old Dongola) and Alodia (Alwa, with its capital at Soba) continued to prosper along the middle Nile, their bishops appointed from Coptic Alexandria and their churches decorated with the striking Nubian frescoes later recovered at Faras. In the Ethiopian highlands the Aksumite state was in its long final decline, its capital gradually shifting south into the Agaw territories where the Zagwe dynasty would emerge two centuries later. Along the East African coast Bantu-speaking populations were laying the foundations of the Swahili city-states.

image.caption: The Djinguereber Mosque at Timbuktu, Mali, built under Mansa Musa in 1327 and one of the great later monuments of the Sudano-Sahelian Islamic tradition whose trans-Saharan trade routes were already active in Ghana's day.

worldContext.text: In the Maya lowlands the prolonged droughts and endemic warfare of the Terminal Classic period were beginning to depopulate the great southern cities; within a century Tikal (which had erected its last dated stela in 869), Palenque, Copan, Yaxchilan, and Piedras Negras would all be effectively abandoned, their monumental construction ceasing and their populations dispersing to the northern Yucatan or into the highlands. The northern Maya cities of the Puuc region, above all Uxmal and Kabah, were entering their florescence, and Chichen Itza was beginning its remarkable ascent. In central Mexico the city of Tula in Hidalgo was emerging as heir to Teotihuacan under the Toltecs, whose plumed-serpent iconography would soon spread as far as Yucatan. On the Peruvian coast and highlands the Wari empire was near collapse, its administrative centres such as Pikillaqta and Viracochapampa being abandoned, and the successor Chimu culture was beginning to organise along the northern coast around Chan Chan. In the American Southwest, Chaco Canyon was just beginning the great pueblo-building era that would produce Pueblo Bonito. Mississippian mound-building was still in its early phase, with Cahokia's great expansion a century away.

image.caption: The Great Plaza and North Acropolis at Tikal in the Peten of Guatemala. The pyramid-temples of Tikal reached their final form in the eighth and ninth centuries before the city's Terminal Classic abandonment.
image.credit: Bjørn Christian Tørrissen, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Charlemagne
figures.role: King of the Franks from 768 and of the Lombards from 774; first western emperor since 476, crowned in Rome on 25 December 800; died 814
figures.credit: Reliquary bust c.1350, Aachen Cathedral Treasury / CC BY-SA 4.0

figures.name: Pope Leo III
figures.role: Bishop of Rome from 795 to 816; performed the coronation after being restored to his see by Charles's intervention

figures.name: Empress Irene
figures.role: Byzantine empress in Constantinople from 797 to 802; her sole rule as a woman was invoked in the west as justification for a new emperor

figures.name: Alcuin of York
figures.role: Northumbrian scholar and abbot of Tours; leading intellectual of the Carolingian Renaissance and adviser to Charles

figures.name: Einhard
figures.role: Frankish courtier and biographer; author of the Vita Karoli Magni, our fullest early account of Charlemagne's life

images.caption: The Coronation of Charlemagne, fresco in the Stanza dell'Incendio di Borgo of the Vatican Palace, designed by Raphael and painted by his workshop around 1516 to 1517.
images.credit: Raphael and workshop, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The reliquary bust of Charlemagne, made in the Mosan region around 1350 and said to contain the top of the emperor's skull. Aachen Cathedral Treasury.
images.credit: Photograph by Florian B. Gutsch, via Wikimedia Commons
